sketches.mechanism: The `object` declaration creates a singleton — exactly one instance can ever exist, and it is initialized lazily the first time it is accessed.
sketches.resonance: Some things in the world exist only once, and you cannot instantiate another; the language guarantees the loneliness of the singular.

sketches.mechanism: The `never` return type indicates a function that can never successfully return — it must throw or loop forever.
sketches.resonance: A promise to stay is a function that can never return; the type system encodes the impossibility of leaving.

sketches.mechanism: Rust's ownership system: calling `drop()` on a value consumes it, and any subsequent attempt to use it is a compile-time error — the value is gone from scope forever.
sketches.resonance: After you let go, the compiler itself forbids you from reaching back; the absence is not a runtime surprise but a structural law.
